title: Plan of the Château de Pierrefonds
creator: After Eugene Viollet-le-Duc; delineator not credited
rightsholder_or_source: Public-domain plate from The Architectural Review vol. 5 (1898/9); via Wikimedia Commons
research_notes: Large clean scan (2018x1198). Plan of a real fortified castle; great for non-orthogonal walls.
research_features: Irregular polygonal curtain-wall castle with multiple large round towers/turrets and curved walls, an inner courtyard, and a keep. Heavy curved-wall and thick-wall geometry, no two rooms alike.
feature_highlights: irregular polygonal curtain wall with non-orthogonal angled segments
multiple large round towers and turrets, some with spiral stairs
curved wall segments linking round towers to straight ranges
great hall and chapel with vaulted, cross-hatched bays
detached outwork/keep across a moat as a separate structure
angled outer site/glacis boundary enclosing the fortress
compass rose (north arrow) and metric scale bar annotations
very thick masonry walls in heavy black poché
supported_examples: straight wall segments at many non-orthogonal angles
thickness-aware walls and wall junctions for the straight ranges
raster underlay with scale calibration to trace the historic plate
roadmap_examples: wall construction profiles / poché for thick masonry curtain walls
spiral/winding stair geometry inside the towers
site/textual metadata for a named historic castle

gap_features.why: the round towers and turrets are true circular structures and the connecting curtain walls bow and curve rather than running straight
a detached outwork/keep with its own corner towers sits apart from the main castle across a moat on the same site
the plan draws the fortified site boundary (glacis/enclosure line) and the moat around the structures, not just the building footprint
the building wraps around a large open inner courtyard (castle ward) enclosed by the ranges
the plate carries a compass rose north arrow and a graphic metric scale bar as drawn annotations

calibration.basis: Provisional: the plate carries a graphic metric scale bar, but it is not yet measured in pixels. This value approximates the fortified site at roughly 150 m across the 1920 px width; refine it against the scale bar before treating the underlay as dimensionally exact.
